items.q: Did the Reformation Parliament make England Protestant?
items.correct: It broke with Rome and made the king head of the Church, but Henry VIII's England kept largely Catholic doctrine and worship.
items.misconception: Henry VIII turned England fully Protestant in the 1530s.
items.random_wrong: The Reformation Parliament abolished the monarchy.
items.why: Students conflate breaking with the Pope with adopting Protestant theology; doctrinal change came mostly later under Edward VI.

items.q: How strong was the crown after Bosworth in 1485?
items.correct: Henry VII secured the throne but faced pretenders and overmighty nobles, working steadily to bolster royal finances and control.
items.misconception: Henry VII was instantly secure with no challenges after 1485.
items.random_wrong: Henry VII ruled jointly with Parliament as equals.
items.why: Learners assume the Tudor crown was immediately dominant; early Tudor monarchs spent years suppressing rivals and rebellions.

items.q: What was Parliament's role under the early Tudors?
items.correct: Parliament was summoned by the monarch and used to legitimise major change, such as the break with Rome, not as a permanent independent body.
items.misconception: Parliament met regularly and limited the crown like a modern legislature.
items.why: Students apply modern ideas; Tudor Parliaments served royal needs and met only when the monarch called them.

items.q: Which 1485 battle founded the Tudor dynasty?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Bosworth. “Towton” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What did the Reformation Parliament of 1529-36 establish?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Royal supremacy over the Church. “The end of the monarchy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What did the Wars of the Roses fight over?
items.correct: The English crown between Lancaster and York
items.why: It's The English crown between Lancaster and York. “Control of France” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What did Henry VIII gain from the Dissolution of the Monasteries?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Their wealth and lands. “A new parliament” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the Battle of Bosworth?
items.why: Exact answer: 1485 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
